=== PLAN ===
Build a browser-based Yew/Rust/WASM p-code VM debugger for pv24a running on COR24 emulator. Two-level debugging: p-code semantic level (primary) and COR24 host drill-down (secondary). Phase 1: scaffold Yew project with Trunk, wire cor24-emulator. Phase 2: control bar + p-code disassembly panel. Phase 3: eval stack + call frame panels. Phase 4: memory region map. Phase 5: host/COR24 drill-down panel. Phase 6: delta highlighting and step-over/step-out. Phase 7: demo registry with embedded .spc test programs.

=== STEPS ===
  [ ] 001-scaffold-yew-project [legacy]: Scaffold the Yew/Rust/WASM project. Create Cargo.toml (edition 2024, Yew 0.21... <-- YOU ARE HERE

Description: Scaffold the Yew/Rust/WASM project. Create Cargo.toml (edition 2024, Yew 0.21...
Task type: rust-project-init

=== PROMPT ===
Scaffold the Yew/Rust/WASM project. Create Cargo.toml (edition 2024, Yew 0.21 CSR, cor24-emulator path dep to ../../sw-embed/cor24-rs, wasm-bindgen, web-sys, gloo). Create index.html with Catppuccin Mocha theme. Create src/main.rs entry point, src/lib.rs with App wrapper, src/debugger.rs with skeleton Debugger component (Component trait, Msg enum with Init/Tick/Reset/StepPcode/StepHost/PauseResume). Create Trunk.toml with unique port. Create scripts/serve.sh and scripts/build-pages.sh. Create build.rs for BUILD_SHA/BUILD_HOST/BUILD_TIMESTAMP. Verify trunk build succeeds. Pattern reference: ~/github/sw-vibe-coding/web-tf24a for debugger structure, ~/github/sw-vibe-coding/web-tml24c for Yew patterns.

=== SKILL: rust-project-init (v1) ===
Create a new Rust project with edition 2024 and strict quality gates

Procedure:
  1. Run cargo init (or cargo new) to create the project
  2. Edit Cargo.toml: set edition = '2024' (NOT 2021)
  3. Verify edition with grep or cat Cargo.toml
  4. Run cargo build to confirm it compiles
  5. Run cargo clippy -- -D warnings to confirm zero warnings
  6. Run cargo fmt --check to confirm formatting

Known failure modes:
  - wrong_edition (5x): Leaving the default edition (2021) instead of setting 2024
  - skip_clippy (2x): Skipping clippy check after init, missing early warnings

=== WHEN DONE ===
Run: agentrail begin    (if not yet started)
Run: agentrail complete --summary "what you did" --next-slug <slug> --next-prompt "next instructions"
  Or: agentrail complete --summary "what you did" --done   (if this is the last step)

file.content:
I want to create a Yew/Rust/WASM web app debugger for my custom p-code VM, written in COR24 assembler.  I have similar debuggers for cor24 .s files and for Forth (implemented in .s and .fth) and for Macro Lisp (implemented in C (which was impemented in Rust) and .l24 lisp). So, how do I take the p-code VM and debug it.  Specifically I'm looking for the kind of displays, of what data, which stack(s), which registers, what memory, how to single step p-code.  Start out simple (no watches, no breakpoints).  I've also implemented TSO Pipeline debuggers (w/watches and breakpoints).    Need ideas for what to build, how it will look, use-cases, and how to make it extensible so I could add watches and break-points.  For now being able to see how memory is used, possibly dis-assemble.  What do I not show?  What makes sense?

You want a Yew/Rust/WASM debugger UI for a custom p-code VM whose implementation lives in COR24 assembler, and you want guidance on:

what to show

how it should look

what data matters

how to single-step p-code rather than only COR24

what not to show initially

how to keep the design extensible for later watches and breakpoints

Given your existing debugger work for COR24 assembly, Forth, Macro Lisp, and even TSO pipeline visualizations, the right move is not to build “just another machine debugger.” Build a two-level debugger with a strong bias toward the p-code semantic level, while still allowing drill-down into the COR24 implementation when needed.

1. High-level architecture
Core idea
Your debugger should treat the system as having three layers:

UI / debugger model layer in Rust/Yew/WASM

VM semantic layer for p-code execution state

host implementation layer for COR24 machine state

That means the debugger is primarily about:

current p-code instruction

p-code PC / frame / stack state

VM-managed memory regions

mapping from p-code step -> underlying COR24 routine(s)

The key design principle is:

Default to showing the p-code machine, not the COR24 machine.
The COR24 state is subordinate and optionally inspectable.

If you do not enforce that, the debugger turns into an assembler debugger with p-code labels painted on top, which is usually the wrong abstraction for debugging compiler/runtime/VM behavior.

2. What problem the debugger is solving
You are debugging at least four different classes of bugs:

A. VM semantic bugs
Examples:

wrong opcode semantics

wrong stack effect

incorrect frame layout

incorrect local-variable addressing

incorrect call/return behavior

bad heap or static data addressing

wrong p-code dispatch

B. p-code program bugs
Examples:

generated p-code is wrong

compiler emits invalid operand order

procedure prologue/epilogue mismatch

incorrect jump targets

C. VM implementation bugs in COR24
Examples:

dispatch loop corrupts registers

VM metadata in memory is wrong

stack pointer update off by one word

byte/word addressing bugs

D. visualization / understanding needs
Examples:

“where is memory going?”

“what frame am I in?”

“why is this return address wrong?”

set -euo pipefail

# Build pages/ for GitHub Pages deployment.
# Run this before committing pages/ changes.

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)"
PROJECT_DIR="$(dirname "$SCRIPT_DIR")"

echo "=== Building pages/ ==="
cd "$PROJECT_DIR"
mkdir -p pages
touch pages/.nojekyll
trunk build --release --public-url /web-dv24r/
rsync -a --delete --exclude='.nojekyll' dist/ pages/

echo "=== Done ==="
echo "Pages built in: $PROJECT_DIR/pages/"
echo "To deploy: git add pages/ && git commit && git push"
